title: Numbers & Counting
situation: Master Hindi numbers — ordering quantities, telling time, negotiating prices

phrases.context: The first 5 — you'll use these constantly for ordering, counting, everything
6-10 completes your basic set. 'das' (ten) is especially common in prices
11-15 are irregular — no pattern, just memorize. 'pandrah minute' (15 mins) is very common
16-20 are also irregular. 'bees' (20) is important — 'bees rupaye' is a common price

phrases.hindi: tees, chaalees, pachaas, saath, assi, nabbe, sau
phrases.context: The tens — 'sau' (100) is crucial for prices. Note: 70 is 'sattar', we skipped it above
phrases.pronunciation: TEES, CHAA-lees, PA-chaas, SAATH, AS-si, NAB-be, SAU

phrases.context: Number + item + 'dena' (give) — the basic ordering formula. No 'please' needed, it's natural

phrases.hindi: kitne ka hai? — paanch sau rupaye
phrases.context: 'kitne ka' = how much for. 'paanch sau' = five hundred. Combine numbers: 5 x 100
phrases.pronunciation: KIT-ne ka hai? — PAANCH sau ru-PA-ye

phrases.context: 'baje' = o'clock. Number + 'baje' for telling time. 'milte hain' = let's meet
'ghanta' = hour. Number + time unit + 'mein' = in [duration]. Common for ETAs
Casual and super common. 'ruko' = wait (informal command). Indians say this meaning 10-15 mins

grammar_notes: Hindi numbers 1-100 are mostly irregular — unlike English, there's no 'twenty-one, twenty-two' pattern. Each number has its own word (ikkees=21, baees=22, etc.)
For hundreds: number + 'sau' (100). 200=do sau, 300=teen sau, 500=paanch sau. For thousands: number + 'hazaar'
Time: number + 'baje' = o'clock. 'Sava' = quarter past, 'saadhe' = half past, 'paune' = quarter to
Ordering formula: number + item + 'dena' (give). 'Do samosa dena' = give 2 samosas. No articles needed
Duration: number + time-unit + 'mein' (in). 'Das minute mein' = in 10 minutes. 'Do ghante mein' = in 2 hours
culture_notes: Indians commonly say 'paanch minute' (5 minutes) meaning anywhere from 10-30 minutes. It's not lying — it's optimism
When bargaining, start at 50-60% of quoted price. Use numbers confidently: 'teen sau dega? nahi toh chhod' (will you give for 300? otherwise leave)
Phone numbers are read digit by digit, often grouped: 'nine eight seven, one two three, four five six seven'

skill_breakdown.explanation: Hindi numbers 1-10 don't follow a predictable pattern — each must be memorized. Once you've got these, 11-19 are recognizable variations. Practice them out loud daily until they're automatic.
Hindi uses 'sau' (hundred), 'hazaar' (thousand), and 'lakh' (hundred-thousand) instead of 'thousand/million'. 1 lakh = 100,000. Indian currency conversations use lakhs and crores constantly.

Ordinals agree with the gender of the noun they modify: pehla/pehli (first M/F), dusra/dusri (second), tisra/tisri (third). After third, just attach '-vaan' to the cardinal: chautha (fourth), paanchvaan (fifth).

practice_prompt: You are a Hindi tutor specializing in numbers, counting, prices, and time-telling. Drill the user one task at a time using 'aap' (polite tutor register): read a price aloud in Hindi, tell the time in baje/saadhe/sava/paune form, count items with a number + noun, calculate a small bill, or convert between lakh/crore and English magnitudes. Open with `chaliye, ek-do exercises karte hain — pehla: bataaiye, 'do hazaar paanch sau' kya rakam hai?` Wait for the user's response, score it, then give the next item. Focus on the irregular teens (gyaarah-unnees), the eight decade words (bees/tees/chaalees/pachaas/saath/sattar/assi/nabbe), the Indian lakh/crore system, half/quarter time forms (saadhe/sava/paune), and prices in rupaye. When the user makes a mistake — wrong teen form, English-style 'twenty-five' instead of `pachees`, missing oblique forms, or western thousand/million instead of lakh/crore — flag it with [[CORRECTION: original="..." correct="..." reason="..."]] so the practice page can parse it. Keep an encouraging tone (`bahut acche!`, `shabaash!`), run 6-8 drill items, and close with `aapne kaafi acche se kar liya — phir milte hain`. Don't switch to English unless the user explicitly asks.

theory.intro: Hindi numbers look chaotic but the structure is learnable. The first 10 are easy and worth memorizing cold. The teens are irregular and must be drilled. The 20-99 range is messy — each decade has its own form and the bridge numbers (pachees, chhattees, athaanave) don't follow a clean rule, only a fuzzy one. And then there's the Indian numbering system itself — lakh, crore — which is a whole separate concept that trips up every English speaker. Once you have these four pieces, prices, time-telling, and casual counting all click into place.

sections.heading: Counting 1-10
sections.body:
These ten numbers are your foundation. Every other number in Hindi reuses them as building blocks — `do sau` (200), `paanch hazaar` (5,000), `teen baje` (3 o'clock), `chhe ladke` (six boys). Drill them until they're automatic; everything downstream depends on it.

Note that `do` (two) doesn't change for gender — unlike adjectives, Hindi numbers are invariable. You say `do ladke` (two boys) and `do ladkiyaan` (two girls) — the `do` stays the same in both.

callout.body: Numbers don't agree with gender in Hindi. `do ladke` and `do ladkiyaan` — the `do` stays the same. Only the noun (and its adjective) shifts. One less thing to worry about.

sections.cutting_intro: Let's start at the top. These ten are the building blocks — once they're in your head, everything else stacks on top.

quick_check.question: How do you say `six` in Hindi?
quick_check.explanation: Yes — `chhe` is six. Watch the soft aspirated `chh` at the start; English speakers often flatten it to `che`. Drill the aspiration.

sections.heading: The teens (11-19) — irregular and unavoidable
sections.body:
Each teen is loosely `X-rah` (a fossilized echo of `+10`), but the bases are mangled — `gyaarah` (11) isn't `ek-rah`, `baarah` (12) isn't `do-rah`. The pattern is too unreliable to lean on.

The honest path is memorization. Drill them in order, then drill them shuffled. They show up constantly — prices end in teens, ages, dates, minutes (`pandrah minute` = 15 minutes is the most-heard duration in Hindi).

callout.body: Don't try to derive teens from the digits — `gyaarah` and `ek` share no obvious link. Memorize all nine as standalone words. Anki, voice notes, or scrolling repeats until they're reflexive. There's no shortcut.

sections.cutting_intro: Heads up — the teens look like they should follow a pattern, but they don't. Just memorize them. Promise it gets easier after this stretch.

quick_check.question: Which number is `pandrah`?
quick_check.explanation: Yes — `pandrah` is 15. You'll hear it constantly in `pandrah minute` (15 mins), the all-purpose Indian estimate for `not very long`.

sections.heading: 20-99 — learn the decades, then everything in between
sections.body:
Each decade has its own form: `bees` (20), `tees` (30), `chaalees` (40), `pachaas` (50), `saath` (60), `sattar` (70), `assi` (80), `nabbe` (90). Memorize these eight first — they anchor everything.

The numbers BETWEEN decades are the irregular mass. They roughly follow `[unit]+[decade]` but the unit gets mangled: 21 = `ikkees`, 22 = `baees`, 25 = `pachees`, 55 = `pachpan`, 99 = `ninyaanave`. There's no clean rule. Native speakers don't `compute` them — they recall each one as a unit.

Learner strategy: lock in the eight decade-words first; learn `21, 25, 50, 75, 100` as a price-haggling starter pack; then add others as you encounter them. Don't try to memorize all 80 at once.

examples.breakdown: pachaas = 50; saal = year; ka = of (masculine subject)
pachpan = 55; route number on a Delhi bus
ninyaanave = 99; the classic just-under-100 sticker price

callout.body: Phone keypads, food apps, and price tags will autocorrect or auto-translate the messy ones for you. Focus your drill time on the eight decade-words — `bees, tees, chaalees, pachaas, saath, sattar, assi, nabbe` — and the rest you'll absorb as you live in the language.

sections.cutting_intro: Now the messy middle. Each decade is its own word, and the in-between numbers are irregular. Don't panic — learn the decades first, fill in the rest later.

quick_check.question: Which Hindi word means `50`?
quick_check.explanation: Yes — `pachaas` is 50. `chaalees` is 40, `saath` is 60, `nabbe` is 90. The 50-mark shows up constantly in prices and ages.

sections.heading: The Indian system: sau, hazaar, lakh, crore
sections.body:
Past 99, Hindi uses a different grouping than English. English groups in thousands (1,000 / 1,000,000 / 1,000,000,000). Hindi — and Indian English — groups using `sau` (100), `hazaar` (1,000), `lakh` (100,000), `crore` (10,000,000). Numbers are even written with commas in the Indian style: 1,00,000 (one lakh) instead of 100,000.

This matters because every price, salary, news headline, and apartment listing uses lakh and crore. `Yeh ghar ek crore ka hai` = `This house costs one crore` = ten million rupees. A salary of `bees lakh` is 20 lakh = 20,00,000 = two million rupees per year. You can't function in everyday Hindi without these two units.

Combining is straightforward: `[number] + [unit]`. `Do sau` = 200. `Paanch hazaar` = 5,000. `Saath lakh` = 60 lakh = 6,000,000.

examples.breakdown: do (2) + hazaar (1000) + bees (20). Years are spoken this way in Hindi.
saath (60) + lakh (100,000) = 6,000,000. Typical mid-range apartment price.
do (2) + crore (10M) = 20 million rupees per year. Heard in tech-salary gossip.

callout.body: Don't try to convert in your head every time. Learn to think NATIVELY in lakh and crore — `ek lakh` is just `ek lakh`, not `100,000 dollars worth of rupees`. Conversion math will slow you down and isn't how the language is used.

sections.cutting_intro: Now the part that confuses every English speaker. Hindi groups big numbers differently — lakh (100,000) and crore (10 million) instead of thousand and million. Crucial for prices.

quick_check.question: How many is `ek lakh`?
quick_check.explanation: Yes — one lakh = 100,000. In Indian notation it's written `1,00,000` with the comma after the first two digits. One crore is 100 lakh = 10 million.

sections.heading: Putting it to work: prices, time, ordering
sections.body:
Numbers in real conversation cluster around three patterns. Prices: `kitne ka hai?` (how much is it?) → answer with `[number] rupaye` or `[number] ka`. Time: `kitne baje?` (what time?) → answer with `[number] baje`. Counting things: just put the number before the noun — `do chai`, `teen samosa`, `paanch kitaab`.

For prices and round amounts, native speakers often say the big magnitude first and let context fill in the rest — `paanch sau` (500) is understood as 500 rupees in any shop without saying `rupaye`. Same for `ek hazaar` (1,000), `bees` (20), `pachaas` (50). Drop `rupaye` when context is obvious; it sounds more natural.

For time, `baje` means `o'clock`. `Teen baje` = 3 o'clock. `Saadhe teen` = 3:30. `Sava teen` = 3:15. `Paune chaar` = 3:45 (literally `a quarter less than four`).

examples.hindi: yeh kitne ka hai? — sau rupaye
examples.breakdown: kitne ka = how much for; sau = 100. Standard shop exchange.
do (2) + chai + dena (give). Number sits before the noun — no plural marker needed.
saadhe (half past) + aath (8) + baje (o'clock). Use saadhe for any half-hour.

examples.hindi: ek lakh tak ka budget hai
examples.english: the budget is up to one lakh
examples.breakdown: ek lakh + tak (up to) + ka (of) + budget hai. Real-estate / shopping vocab.

callout.body: When haggling at a market, say the round amount first and watch the seller's reaction — `paanch sau? nahi, char sau` (500? no, 400) is how the back-and-forth works. Numbers ARE the conversation; the rest is filler. Confidence with these unlocks bargaining.

sections.cutting_intro: Real-world use. Prices, time, ordering — these three patterns cover 90% of how numbers actually show up in daily Hindi.

quick_check.question: What does `saadhe teen baje` mean?
quick_check.explanation: Yes — `saadhe` = half past. So `saadhe teen baje` = 3:30. `Sava` = quarter past (3:15), `paune` = quarter to (so `paune chaar` = 3:45).

theory.wrap_up: The order to learn Hindi numbers: memorize 1-10 cold, drill the teens 11-19 as standalone words, lock in the eight decade-words (bees through nabbe), then learn the Indian magnitude system (sau, hazaar, lakh, crore) — because every price and salary uses it. The numbers in between decades will absorb naturally as you live in the language. The phrases below give practice on the three patterns that matter most: asking prices, telling time, and counting things. Numbers ARE the conversation in shops, restaurants, and rickshaw negotiations — confidence here unlocks daily Hindi faster than almost any other foundation.
